target.name: Mainspring Energy
target.founder: Shannon Miller, Adam Simpson, Matt Svrcek

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series E → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.4x) to $400M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 21 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Mainspring Energy is a Series E climate and energy company that has raised $400M+ to date. The valuation framework draws on a limited peer universe: one public comparable (LNZA) and eight private peers in the Climate & Energy sector, including TAE Technologies, Redwood Materials, Helion, Commonwealth Fusion Systems, and QuantumScape. However, no same-stage or adjacent-stage peers with sufficient similarity exist to support a robust peer-median valuation approach.

Given data constraints, the analysis relies on stage-progression methodology, which applies a typical 1.4x step-up multiplier to the $400M total-raised proxy as a baseline, yielding $560M. This method reflects observed capital-raising cadence for Series E companies in the sector but does not incorporate peer-relative multiples due to insufficient comparable data at similar stages and valuations.

The triangulation yields a valuation range of $476M (low) to $644M (high), with a median of $560M. The range reflects ±15% sensitivity around the stage-progression result. Key uncertainties include the absence of direct peer multiples—particularly equity-value-to-revenue or capital-to-stage ratios for same-stage peers—and reliance on aggregate capital raised as a valuation proxy rather than operational metrics. A refined valuation would benefit from additional private comps at Series D–F stages or comparable operational data (ARR, unit economics, runway).
